title: Honey castile cold-process soap
subtitle: A high-olive castile bar with honey added at trace for a soft golden colour
excerpt: A 100% olive oil castile cold-process bar with a teaspoon of honey added at trace for a creamier lather and golden hue. Castile bars are gentle but slow to lather; the honey contribution is small but real. Allow 8 weeks to harden before use; castile rewards a long cure.
sourceNotes: Cavitch, The Soapmaker's Companion (1997): castile method and cure times. Honey at 1 tsp per 500 g oils is the standard cold-process dosing; higher amounts overheat the bar.

recipe.yieldDescription: 1 kg bar soap (~7 x 130 g bars after cure)
recipe.batchNotes: Doubles cleanly. Castile traces slowly so larger batches are forgiving.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Castile rewards a long cure. The bar is useable at 8 weeks, better at 12, and very fine at 6 months. Stockpile bars to age.
recipe.shelfLifeNotes: Stable 2 years or more cured and stored cool. The honey can darken the bar slightly over time; this is normal. Discard if orange spots appear or the bar smells sharp.

glossaryTerms.definition: A soap made with 100% olive oil. The classification dates to medieval Castile in Spain. Castile bars are mild and slow-tracing, give a low lather (almost a slip rather than bubbles), and reward a long cure. A bar cured 6 months is a different bar from one cured 8 weeks; commercial castile is often aged a year.

Honey at 1 tsp per 500 g oils added at trace contributes natural sugars that boost lather and trace minerals that give a soft golden hue. Higher amounts overheat the bar during saponification and can scorch the colour to dark brown. Add at light trace, fully blended, before pouring.

content.text: A high-olive bar is a mild bar. This is a

- 100% olive oil - with a small amount of honey added at trace for a golden tint and a slightly creamier lather. Castile is the original soapmaker's bar: mild enough for baby skin, slow to lather, slow to dissolve, slow to cure. The reward is a soap that suits anyone who finds commercial bars stripping.
recipe at 5% superfat and 38% lye discount (more water than a standard bar; castile benefits from the longer working time). Cure 8 weeks minimum, 12 weeks ideal. The
goes in at light trace; do not exceed 1 teaspoon per 500 g oils or the bar overheats and discolours during curing.

: safety goggles, nitrile gloves, long sleeves, closed shoes. Pour lye into water, never water into lye. Ventilate. Cold tap running ready to flush. The honey-added batter heats more than a base castile during the first 24 hours in the mould; do not insulate heavily or you may get gel-overheat cracks. A folded tea towel rather than a heavy blanket.

items.prepNote: Pomace, refined, or extra-virgin all work. Pomace traces fastest; extra-virgin gives the most distinctive olive aroma in the cured bar.
5% superfat calculation for 1000 g olive oil. Weigh to 1 g precision.
Distilled. 38% lye discount; gives a longer trace window on a slow-tracing oil.
About 1 teaspoon. Any liquid honey; mild floral varieties (acacia, clover) give the cleanest scent. Pre-warm slightly so it pours; do not heat above 40 degrees Celsius.
1.5% of oils. Optional. Pairs with honey for a clean floral. Skip for a pure unscented castile.

content.text: Goggles and gloves on. Weigh the distilled water into the HDPE pitcher. Weigh the sodium hydroxide separately. Pour the lye into the water a tablespoon at a time, stirring, until dissolved. The solution heats to 80-90 degrees Celsius. Set aside and cool to 35-38 degrees Celsius. Castile prefers cooler temperatures than a standard bar.

Weigh the olive oil into a stainless or heatproof glass bowl. Warm gently if cold (room temperature is fine; do not melt above 40 degrees Celsius). Aim to match the lye temperature within 5 degrees Celsius.

Pour the lye solution into the oil through a fine strainer. Pulse the stick blender in 5-second bursts, stirring between. Castile traces slowly: expect 8-15 minutes of pulsing. The batter goes from translucent to pale yellow to opaque cream over that time. At light trace (a drizzled spoon leaves a faint trail), warm the honey in a small bowl and stir in by hand. The batter will deepen slightly to a warm gold. Add the lavender essential oil if using.

Pour into the silicone loaf mould. Tap to release air. Cover with a folded tea towel - lightly, not insulated. Rest 48 hours; castile takes longer to firm up than a coconut-heavy bar. Unmould when firm; cut into 7 x 130 g bars. Stand on a wire rack in a cool airy spot. Turn weekly. Cure 8 weeks minimum, 12 weeks ideal. The bar will be soft and sticky in the first 4 weeks, then hardens steadily.

Wrap each bar in waxed paper after the cure. 2 years cool and dry. The honey may darken the bar to a deeper gold over the months; this is normal. Discard if orange spots appear (DOS, rancid superfat) or the bar smells sharp.
